item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can very much unsee it XD. I wonder if there's other SCPs that can be compared to Pokémon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh god I can't unsee it now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, but i felt like there was something missing with how the foundation found out about the whole command words thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It had my attention at the description. It had my full attention at the list of post-eaten, converted materials.

It had all the upvotes when I got to the graffiti. Roman graffiti is the best thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. The graffiti content makes it all the more authentic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Ptolemy was here".

I'd chalk that up to it being someone completely different who just happened to be named "Ptolemy".

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ptolemy_%28name%29

The name was a lot more common than you seem to think; there's a reason that the name "Bartholomew" ('son of Ptolemy') exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My initial thought
without the eyebrows, of course

But you did this very well. I like it a lot. Roman graffiti is brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoa O.o I like this article. It makes sense to me that the Romans would love to have these things. Personally, the origins and other potential issues with the article don't bother me at all. It's well written and captivating. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yep

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1589 does not have a head: the subject’s eyes, ear canals, nostrils and mouth are all located on the torso and abdomen.

Did anyone else think of Strong Mad here? :-D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that wonderful bit of graffiti is because some guy in the Second Valiant named Lucius forgot to turn off his swag one night and woke up covered in bitches, and to rub it in everyone's face he carved it into their Man-Eater.

Or he was full of shit and did it to fool future members of the legion into believing that such a scenario had happened.

the way you phrased that was worth the price of admission all on its own

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My Latin's a bit rusty, but I think it should be Magni, not Magnae.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
doesn't reference any knowledge of others

There's a IV branded on the left shoulder. Just sayin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I spent my time at work thinking about this and basically my issue comes down entirely to me generating my own headcanon while reading it.

See, this thing would be incredibly useful in any war in the modern day; it is a walking battlefield clearance, fortification constructing and soldier feeding factory that can defend itself handily. Back in ye olden roman(e?) times, it would've literally been a walking miracle.

Due to the fact that the foundation only has one in containment and the article doesn't reference any knowledge of others, I'm assuming that either this is the only one in existence OR it is/was an extraordinarily rare creature.

With that in mind, in my head, this thing would've been guarded more heavily than anything else on the battlefield. Even if they believed it was fully capable of defending itself against any number of enemy soldiers, the risk of it wandering off, falling off a cliff, getting stolen or whatever is too high to risk. It would've been treated like super royalty, or like a huge chunk of diamond encrusted gold. So with that in mind, the idea that someone could manage to scrawl "dude I fucked some wenches and it was rad" on it just seems amazingly bizarre to me.

But I'm fully aware that this is purely headcanon on my part, so my upvote remains, and this is more explanation of my view than any real criticism. It's just as likely that this thing came from some alternate dimension where every roman legion had a few of these things kicking around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First I had to google a bit to remember the word "blemmyes". Then I had to upvote you for making one. Good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The monster itself was not all that interesting (though having seen the mythohistorical context, I may have to rethink that opinion), but I upvoted for the graffiti. And up until the last addendum explained more or less how it got there, I was wondering why this wasn't University material. No one uses the University anymore, sadface.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never knew that the comma in Othello actually marked a different between the two descriptions.

That's pretty confusing. I guess Keith Thompsons Anthrophagus isn't very correct anymore. Aw.

But I love this either way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I highly doubt that they did it out in the open: maybe some of the half-completed ones, yeah, but most were crafty.

After a while it became sort of like decals painted on bombers, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this because I found it awesome.

That said, the graffiti bugs me. Not the content itself, which is pretty spot on roman stuff, but the fact that there's graffiti on it at all. I don't know why that bugs me, but it does. The official insignias and such are fine, but random soldiers writing stuff on it sets off an alarm in my head. I just envision a commander walking around and seeing a random soldier carving swear words into their (presumably very rare) mobile corpse processing unit and getting quite annoyed.

I ignored that though because I'm absolutely positive I'm wrong. "Soldiers don't graffiti on things!", says my brain, "they're disciplined and well behaved at all times!" Shut up, brain, you don't know jack.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not ideal, but better. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Blemmyes you did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, if someone can tattoo one of those phrases onto an elephant and take a picture of it, I would be most appreciative.

Facetiousness aside, that's another comment for it, so removing pic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Classic Romans.

Seconding that the picture is totally random. Like, the Foundation looked at this incredible monstrous thing and thought, "You know what will help people recognise this? A plaster cast of the bottom of one of its feet."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know. It's just that this one cracked me up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was somewhat neat… and then the graffiti made me full on upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Roman graffiti is some of the most prominent and boastful in the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… it's some sort of living, ancient, golem-like reprocessing plant? I love the idea!
The picture seems a bit random. I'd like to see the scars in latin, or something like that.

Noscendus est Lucius hac nocte multas pulchras feminas futuit. (Let it be known: Lucius fucked many beautiful women this night) – Located on the left abdominal side.

Oh wow xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Yes I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You wrote a blemmyes into an SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have addressed your points: it doesn't eat clothes, and it survived by being underground. Tomb or cave-in, take your pick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpicking, here:
Pellets of salt and iron are a little ambigious as to whether they are pellets composed of both salt and iron, or pellets of salt and pellets of iron are produced. I assume iron is meant to be either from blood or from metal objects worn by the corpse. The second makes wonder what happens to other nonorganics, or clothing- is it removed, consumed (I assume this), and if so, excreted? (There are unspecified orifices, of course). This in turn leads me to wonder- if he recently ate someone, he likely encountered modern synthetic materials. Might be something in how those are handled.
I'm a little confused as to how a living thing is able to survive for so long, esp. with a period of hard wear (Battle), but tattoos and scars are still legible 2000 years later.

Still, I like this one. I especially like how similar this manages to be to Fernand in terms of substance while being completely different in every other way.

item.title: Re: Upvoted, but with a caveat.
item.content:encoded: Oh yeah, the soldiers working with it would know the difference. It's only referenced as an anthropophagus outside the article proper, so consider it a lazy researcher in-universe, or the actual author going by the sound of the word outside of universe.

item.title: Re: Upvoted, but with a caveat.
item.content:encoded: At least you didn't call it a sciapod. Then I would shove my fist down your throat. But I would like to think that the Romans would know the difference between the two, seeing as Pliny the Elder's work was still relatively recent at the time.

item.title: Re: Upvoted, but with a caveat.
item.content:encoded: Blemmyes is more accurate, yes, but it doesn't sound nearly as cool. It still eats dudes, though, so it still fits.

item.title: Upvoted, but with a caveat.
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, but… this isn't an anthropophage. Anthropophagi are a race of cannibals that, by all accounts, look like perfectly normal human beings. This is, in fact, a blemmyes. I will concede, however, that there isn't much difference, as they are both cannibalistic, but still.

The mix-up of anthropophagi and blemmyes comes from this line in Shakespeare's Othello:

And of the Cannibals that each other eat,
The Anthropophagi, and men whose heads
Do grow beneath their shoulders.

Now you know.
And knowing is half the battle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I've been waiting for an anthropophagus! They're one of my favorite monsters. Thanks for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed the containment protocols a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm neutral voting for now, just because I feel the containment procedures need to be reevaluated a bit. It's an intelligent creature - it might not be sapient, but it possesses the ability to use tools, and just because it seems like it isn't going to break out of containment, doesn't mean they shouldn't prepare for the worst.

Otherwise, I really like the concept. I like the idea of what's basically a battlefield janitor, and I like it even better that it seems to be natural. Although, I don't quite understand if it's suppose to be stone or organic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, that's better. It really isn't a big deal, it's just me being nitpicky about Roman history, as I'm prone to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm…I added a stick figure to it, so it's more of a "Kilroy was here" thing (like I had originally intended). Some guy somewhere heard the name Ptolemy, liked the sound of it, and turned it into a meme. Does that work better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered that, but that opens a whole new set of historical questions. For that legionnaire to really know who Ptolemy actually was well enough to be a smartass about it, he would need to be fairly well educated, especially for a post Marian Reform legionnaire (by 105 most of the Roman army was comprised of the rural proletarian, traditionally the least educated folks in the Empire).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture was there only because I wanted a picture, so that'll get chopped if someone else brings it up.

The addendum is just an account of origin, nothing more, and that wonderful bit of graffiti is because some guy in the Second Valiant named Lucius forgot to turn off his swag one night and woke up covered in bitches, and to rub it in everyone's face he carved it into their Man-Eater.

Or he was full of shit and did it to fool future members of the legion into believing that such a scenario had happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would chalk it up to a legionnaire being a smartass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My Latin is really far too poor to make any solid suggestion on that front. I do, however, notice what seems to be a slight historical blunder:

"(Ptolemy was here)"- The Ptolemaic dynasty was the ruler of Egypt from the death of Alexander and until the death of Cleopatra (30 BC), more than a hundred years before Trajan (circa 105 AD). It makes no real sense for this name to appear on the Man-Eater, unless we have an undead Hellenistic Pharaoh on our hands here, or an unusually well-bred legionnaire.

As for the SCP itself, I'm not quite sure what to make of it. I'll have another read of it later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always wondered how they kept those Roman battlefields so neat and free of pesky dead bodies!

Seriously, I like this. A useful, albeit huge organic machine to convert dead bodies to nutritious food and rock into usable products. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To me, what sets this apart from other SCPs is that it's purely militarily functional; a magical Roman version of a bomb-sniffing robot or something. Which is cool, but isn't a very exciting hook. It feels like something is missing. I'm sure there's some aspect of this SCP, either in the article or just in your headcanon, that you could play up a bit to tie it together a little more.

Man, I feel a bit like a little kid trying to help mum cook or something. Regardless of whatever changes you do or don't make, I like thinking about the adventures of the Second Valiant Trajanic Legion. That'd be a cool Tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good. When I first saw it and saw something like 'Novomundus' I thought it would be an Alexylva University artefact though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm fairly sure that's a direct translation of Roman graffiti found in Pompeii.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't get so salty; I love the idea, but some of the choices were a little odd. Why would the Foundation add a plaster cast of a Bigfoot fake as a visual representation of what this creature looks like? What's the point of the addendum aside from the point of origin?

Let it be known: Lucius fucked many beautiful women this night

And what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. Those were totally my rolls here. It wasn't as if I was trying to write something that had (gasp!) originally been made for a practical purpose or anything, no, that would be silly and stupid. Just like funny things, practical things never happen in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"D20, what should my SCP be based on?!"
You roll a 1(+0)! Critical miss!
Your SCP now looks like Gossamer!
"D20, what should my SCP do?!"
You roll a 2(+0)! Critical miss!
Your SCP now eats dead bodies and rocks!
"D20, give me a personality quirk to make my SCP likeable!"
You roll a 2(+0)! Critical miss!
Your SCP is now covered in Latin graffiti!

I don't wanna play this game anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything you need is in the article, really. A few Google searches will clear most things up.

+ Alternatively...
- you spoiler-reader

This thing is an anthropophagus (a man-eater), very possibly artificial in nature (as it's marked as the product of a factory), was either the fourth of its series or the fourth attached to the Second Valiant Trajanic Legion (which was stationed in Alexandria, explaining why it was discovered in Egypt).

And it seems the boys were fond of it, sort of like a mascot, affectionately calling it "The Elephant".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I'm missing something, but I like this anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it a lot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I said in chat. Reminds me of LotR troll too much, doesn't overly grab me. But a neat enough little SCP. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've had this one sitting around for months, here goes nothing.

e: I can guarantee that something was borked with the Latin, so anyone with the know (Dmatix, looking at you), go ahead and fix.
